rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ERNEST V. RHINE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Kimberly Rhine Gagosian To Bifurcate Or Sever For Early Evidentiary Hearing, In The Pending Trust Contest Action Brought On The Ground Of Undue Influence, The Issues Of Lack Of Active Participation And Lack Of Undue Benefit
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The instant trust and estate matters have been consolidated for purposes of discovery and trial. The consolidated matters involve, among other things, undue-influence challenges to three testamentary instruments: the 2005 will, the 2004 trust amendment and the 2005 trust amendment. Respondent Kimberley Rhine Gagosian has filed a Motion to Bifurcate or Sever for Early Evidentiary Hearing, in the Pending Trust Contest Action Brought on the Ground of Undue Influence, the Issues of Lack of Active Participation and Lack of Undue Benefit ("Issue Bifurcation Motion"). The motion is DENIED. Preliminarily, the court sees no reason to disturb the order consolidating the matters. Respondent's Issue Bifurcation Motion is made pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 598 and section 1048, subdivision (b), both of which allow a court in its discretion to separately try issues, even issues within claims, when the court finds that a separate trial would promote convenience, economy and fairness. Here, on the current showing, the court finds that separately trying a subset of the elements of the undue influence claims makes no sense, would be inconsistent with the law of undue influence and, in fact, would risk substantial unfairness in the proceedings. There is no material showing that the convenience of witnesses, the ends of justice, or the economy and efficiency of handling the litigation would be promoted by bifurcating or severing two of the three necessary elements of the initial evidentiary showing, regarding the challenge to only one of the three testamentary instruments. Moreover, an undue influence claim "need not be supported by direct evidence of undue influence at the moment decedent signed the trust instruments" (Lintz v. Lintz (2014) 222 Cal. App. 4th 1346, 1355), nor must the determination of undue influence in procuring a testamentary instrument depend on any one element alone, but on the "probative force" of the elements in combination (Lingenfelter's Estate (1952) 38 Cal.2d 571, 585; Estate of Baker (1982) 131 Cal.App.3d 471, 481). Thus, the proposed issue bifurcation risks substantial unfairness in the proceedings. The court finds that these concerns substantially outweigh any factors that may favor bifurcation and declines to exercise its discretion to order bifurcation of issues as Respondent proposes in her motion. Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on February 11, 2020.
